summary: Gruber critiques Facebook's iOS app for two UI failures: being embarrassingly late to support Dark Mode (over a year after iOS 13 introduced it) and getting disclosure chevrons wrong by following Android conventions instead of iOS platform idioms. He explains that iOS/Mac disclosure chevrons correctly indicate state (right = collapsed, down = expanded), while Facebook/Android chevrons indicate action (down = tap to expand, up = tap to collapse). He argues the Mac/iOS approach of showing state and implying action is superior to showing action and implying state. The post also takes a characteristic swipe at Facebook's broader inability to prioritize craft and great design.
key_points: Facebook only began rolling out Dark Mode for iOS over a year after iOS 13 introduced it, which Gruber calls embarrassing
Facebook uses Android-style disclosure chevrons that indicate action rather than iOS-style chevrons that indicate state, breaking a core platform idiom
The Mac/iOS convention of chevrons showing state dates back to 1984 and is the superior design approach
Even Microsoft eventually abandoned its poor +/- button design and adopted Mac-style state-indicating triangles
Facebook's disregard for platform conventions reflects its broader indifference to making great things
money_quotes: I resisted the urge and Facebook let Paper wither on the vine because making great things has never been their game.

Given that Dark Mode was introduced in iOS 13 over a year ago, and is both incredibly popular and a wonderful accessibility feature for many people, how is this only rolling out now? That's embarrassing.
I noticed immediately — and was completely unsurprised — that Facebook gets their disclosure chevrons for hiding/showing hierarchical content wrong.
I'll go to the mat arguing that disclosure chevrons should show state and imply action (the Mac/iOS way), not show action and imply state (the Android/Facebook way).
It's undeniably wrong for an iOS app — they're breaking a core platform idiom.
Windows used to use ridiculous little +/- buttons for this because of course Windows started with the worst possible UI.
Anyway, this is making me think I should never join Facebook.
key_insight: Disclosure chevrons should indicate state rather than action — a platform convention Facebook ignores on iOS, revealing its indifference to design craft and platform respect.
